epigraph.text: He made the moon to mark the seasons; the sun knows its time for setting.

content.text:
A tangent line grazes a circle at exactly one point—touching it 
without crossing into its interior. This seemingly simple relationship 
gives rise to elegant geometric properties. Tangent lines appear 
throughout mathematics, physics, and engineering: in the paths of 
objects leaving circular orbits, in the design of gears and cams, 
and in the analysis of curves in calculus.

content.title: Tangent Line
content.content:
A **tangent line** (or simply **tangent**) 
to a circle is a line that intersects the circle at exactly one point. 
This point is called the **point of tangency**.

content.text:
In contrast, recall that a **secant** is a line that intersects 
a circle at two points, and a line that does not intersect the circle 
at all is called an **external line**.
The fundamental property of tangent lines relates them to radii.

content.title: Tangent-Radius Theorem
content.content:
A line is tangent to a circle if and only if it is perpendicular 
to the radius at the point of tangency.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Proof sketch:* Consider a tangent line $\ell$ at point $P$ on 
circle $O$. Let $Q$ be any other point on $\ell$. Since $\ell$ is tangent, 
$Q$ is outside the circle, so $OQ > OP$ (the radius). This means $P$ is 
the point on $\ell$ closest to $O$. But the shortest distance from a 
point to a line is along the perpendicular. Therefore $\overline{OP} 
\perp \ell$.

content.title: Using the Tangent-Radius Theorem
content.solution:
By the Tangent-Radius Theorem, $\overline{OP} \perp \ell$, so 
$\angle OPA = 90°$. Triangle $OPA$ is a right triangle with legs 
$OP = 5$ and $PA = 12$.

By the Pythagorean Theorem:
$$
 OA^2 &= OP^2 + PA^2 \\
 OA^2 &= 5^2 + 12^2 \\
 OA^2 &= 25 + 144 = 169 \\
 OA &= 13
$$

content.title: Finding the tangent length
content.problem:
From external point $A$, a line is tangent to circle $O$ at point 
$P$. If $OA = 17$ and the radius of the circle is 8, find $AP$.
content.solution:
Since $\overline{OP} \perp \overline{AP}$, triangle $OPA$ is a right 
triangle with hypotenuse $OA = 17$ and leg $OP = 8$.

$$
 AP^2 + OP^2 &= OA^2 \\
 AP^2 + 64 &= 289 \\
 AP^2 &= 225 \\
 AP &= 15
$$

content.text: Tangent Segments from an External Point

content.title: Tangent Segment
content.content:
A **tangent segment** is a segment from 
an external point to the point of tangency on a circle.

content.text:
From any external point, exactly two tangent lines can be drawn to 
a circle. The tangent segments to these two points of tangency have 
a remarkable property.

content.title: Two-Tangent Theorem
content.content: Tangent segments from the same external point are congruent.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Proof:* Let $A$ be an external point, and let $P$ and $Q$ be 
the points of tangency of the two tangent lines from $A$ to circle $O$.

Draw radii $\overline{OP}$ and $\overline{OQ}$. By the Tangent-Radius 
Theorem:
By the Hypotenuse-Leg Theorem, $\triangle OPA \cong \triangle OQA$.

content.title: Using the Two-Tangent Theorem
content.problem:
Two tangent segments from point $A$ to a circle have lengths 
$3x + 5$ and $5x - 1$. Find $x$ and the length of each tangent segment.
content.solution:
By the Two-Tangent Theorem, the segments are equal:
$$
 3x + 5 &= 5x - 1 \\
 6 &= 2x \\
 x &= 3
$$

Each tangent segment has length:
\[3(3) + 5 = 14 \qquad \text{or} \qquad 5(3) - 1 = 14\]

content.title: Circumscribed Polygon
content.content:
A polygon is **circumscribed** about 
a circle if each side of the polygon is tangent to the circle. The 
circle is called an **inscribed circle** or **incircle**.

content.text: The Two-Tangent Theorem is especially useful for circumscribed polygons.

content.title: Circumscribed quadrilateral
content.solution:
Let the circle touch $\overline{AB}$ at $P$, $\overline{BC}$ at $Q$, 
$\overline{CD}$ at $R$, and $\overline{DA}$ at $S$.

By the Two-Tangent Theorem:

- $AP = AS$ (tangents from $A$)

- $BP = BQ$ (tangents from $B$)

- $CQ = CR$ (tangents from $C$)

- $DR = DS$ (tangents from $D$)

Let $AP = AS = a$, $BP = BQ = b$, $CQ = CR = c$, and $DR = DS = d$.

Then:
$$
 AB &= AP + PB = a + b = 12 \\
 BC &= BQ + QC = b + c = 9 \\
 CD &= CR + RD = c + d = 14 \\
 DA &= DS + SA = d + a = \, ?
$$

Adding the first and third equations: $a + b + c + d = 12 + 14 = 26$.

Adding the second and fourth equations: $b + c + d + a = 9 + DA$.

Therefore: $9 + DA = 26$, so $DA = 17$.

Alternatively, note that for any circumscribed quadrilateral:
\[AB + CD = BC + DA\]
\[12 + 14 = 9 + DA\]
\[DA = 17\]

content.title: Circumscribed Quadrilateral Theorem
content.content:
If a quadrilateral is circumscribed about a circle, the sums of 
opposite sides are equal:
\[AB + CD = BC + DA\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text: Two circles can have up to four common tangent lines.

content.title: Common Tangents
content.content:
A **common tangent** is a line tangent to 
two circles. An **internal common tangent** crosses between the 
circles. An **external common tangent** does not cross between them.

content.text: The number of common tangents depends on the position of the circles:

items: **2 tangents**: circles intersect at two points
**0 tangents**: one circle is inside the other

content.title: Common tangent length
content.solution:
Draw the external common tangent touching circle 1 at $P$ and circle 2 
at $Q$. Draw radii $O_1P = 5$ and $O_2Q = 3$, which are perpendicular 
to the tangent.

From $O_2$, draw a line parallel to $\overline{PQ}$ meeting $O_1P$ extended 
at point $R$. Then $O_2RPQ$ forms a rectangle (the sides $O_1P$ and $O_2Q$ 
are parallel since both are perpendicular to $PQ$).

So $O_1R = O_1P - RP = 5 - 3 = 2$ and $O_1O_2 = 12$.

In right triangle $O_1RO_2$:
$$
 RO_2^2 + O_1R^2 &= O_1O_2^2 \\
 RO_2^2 + 4 &= 144 \\
 RO_2^2 &= 140 \\
 RO_2 &= \sqrt{140} = 2\sqrt{35}
$$

Since $RPQO_2$ is a rectangle, $PQ = RO_2 = 2\sqrt{35}$.

marginNotes.text:
It is he who sits above the circle of 
the earth, and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers.

He has inscribed a circle on the face of 
the waters at the boundary between light and darkness.

The appearance of the wheels was like 
the gleaming of beryl. And their construction was as if a wheel were 
within a wheel.
